topic: An employee is transferred to a different department
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the weight of a stone. Transfer. To Marketing. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent lights of the office suddenly too bright, too unforgiving. Sarah, my direct supervisor for the last three years, had explained it with a practiced, corporate smile, but I could feel the tightening in my chest, a cold, heavy pressure spreading outward. When I finally gathered my things, my movements were jerky, almost clumsy. Every glance from a colleague felt like a spotlight, dissecting the awkwardness of my sudden relocation. I kept my gaze fixed on the carpet, trying to shrink into the beige cubicle walls, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 2]

"So, the big move," Mark said, clapping me on the shoulder with an energy that felt entirely misplaced. I managed a tight, practiced smile that didn't reach my eyes. Inside, a strange hollowness echoed. It felt like someone had just taken a piece of my confidence and placed it on a shelf, out of sight. I watched the other team members gather around him, their easy laughter sounding like a foreign language. I just nodded, my throat suddenly dry, focusing intently on the pattern of the cheap laminate desk. Every step toward the elevator felt like wading through thick mud.

[story 3]

I walked into the new department’s open-plan area, and the sheer volume of unfamiliar faces was overwhelming. My hands were clammy as I clutched my worn messenger bag strap. The air smelled different—stronger, sharper—and the conversations around me were rapid-fire, jargon-heavy exchanges I couldn't decipher. I tried to make eye contact with someone I vaguely recognized, offering a small, tentative wave. When they didn't return it, the brief connection shattered. I felt a flush rise up my neck, hot and undeniable, and I quickly turned away, focusing on the distant window, wishing I could become invisible.
